trade.summary: Moderate bilateral trade. Israel exports pharmaceuticals, hi-tech equipment, and chemicals; Sweden exports machinery, pharmaceuticals, and manufactured goods. Trade relations are separate from the political tensions.

diplomatic.summary: Sweden recognized the State of Palestine in 2014, the first EU member to do so, creating significant bilateral friction. Relations worsened further during the Gaza conflict with the Swedish government and public strongly critical of Israeli military operations. Sweden called for a ceasefire, supported ICC proceedings, and imposed arms export restrictions. Sweden's foreign minister has been among the most vocal European critics of Israel.
recent_incidents: Sweden supported ICJ proceedings against Israel

military.summary: No formal military cooperation. Sweden (now NATO member) imposed arms export restrictions on Israel during the Gaza conflict. Intelligence cooperation within broader Western frameworks has limited application to bilateral ties.
incidents: Sweden suspended arms exports to Israel 2024

financial.summary: Swedish institutional investors hold Israeli bonds and equities. Some Swedish pension funds have divested from Israeli defense companies. Bilateral investment in tech and pharma continues at commercial level. Ericsson and other Swedish companies have Israeli operations.

energy.summary: No significant bilateral energy trade. Sweden's energy mix is primarily renewable and nuclear; no dependency on Israeli energy.

scientific.summary: Active scientific cooperation despite political tensions. Swedish and Israeli researchers collaborate in life sciences, climate technology, and computer science. The Karolinska Institute has partnerships with Israeli medical schools. Political tensions have not fully disrupted academic channels.
